  2. [CrCl2 (en)2]Cl
question_text: Name the following coordination compounds according to IUPAC norms: [Co (NH 3 ) 4 (H 2 O)Cl]Cl 2 [CrCl 2 (en) 2 ]Cl
explanation: IUPAC name of the following coordination compounds are: [Co(NH 3 ) 4 (H 2 O)Cl] Cl 2 : Tetra ammine aquachloridocobalt (III) chloride [Cr Cl 2 (en) 2 ] Cl: Dichlorido bis-(ethane-1,2- diamine) chromium (Ill) chloride.
solution: IUPAC name of the following coordination compounds are: [Co(NH 3 ) 4 (H 2 O)Cl] Cl 2 : Tetra ammine aquachloridocobalt (III) chloride [Cr Cl 2 (en) 2 ] Cl: Dichlorido bis-(ethane-1,2- diamine) chromium (Ill) chloride.
solution_html:
IUPAC name of the following coordination compounds are:

  1. [Co(NH3)4(H2O)Cl] Cl2: Tetra ammine aquachloridocobalt (III) chloride
  2. [Cr Cl2(en)2] Cl: Dichlorido bis-(ethane-1,2- diamine) chromium (Ill) chloride.

  1. [Co(NH3)6]3+
  2. [Ni(CO)4]

[At. no. : Co = 27, Ni = 28]
question_text: Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of the following: [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [Ni(CO) 4 ] [At. no. : Co = 27, Ni = 28]
explanation: The hybridization and magnetic character of [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is d 2 sp 3 and diamagnetic The hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CO) 4 ] is sp 3 and diamagnetic.
solution: The hybridization and magnetic character of [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is d 2 sp 3 and diamagnetic The hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CO) 4 ] is sp 3 and diamagnetic.
solution_html:
1. The hybridization and magnetic character of [Co(NH3)6]3+ is d2sp3 and diamagnetic
  2. The hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CO)4] is sp3 and diamagnetic.

    1. {tex}\left. \mid {Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)({CN})({en})_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\left.\mid {PtCl}_{4}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\left. \mid {Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}({ONO})\right]^{+}{/tex}

question_text: Write IUPAC names of the following: {tex}\left. \mid {Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)({CN})({en})_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex} {tex}\left.\mid {PtCl}_{4}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left. \mid {Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}({ONO})\right]^{+}{/tex} What is spectrochemical series? Write the difference between a strong field ligand and a weak field ligand.
explanation: Aquacyanidobis (ethane-1, 2-diamine) cobalt (III) ion Tetrachloridoplatinate(II) ion Tetraamminechloridonitrito-O-chromium (III) ion The arrangement of ligands in increasing order of field strength is called spectrochemical series /it is an experimentally determined series based on the absorption of light by complexes with different ligands. {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt P{/tex} , weak field ligand; {tex}\Delta_{0} \gt P{/tex} , strong field ligand/Weak ligand form high spin complexes whereas strong field form low spin complexes.
solution: Aquacyanidobis (ethane-1, 2-diamine) cobalt (III) ion Tetrachloridoplatinate(II) ion Tetraamminechloridonitrito-O-chromium (III) ion The arrangement of ligands in increasing order of field strength is called spectrochemical series /it is an experimentally determined series based on the absorption of light by complexes with different ligands. {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt P{/tex} , weak field ligand; {tex}\Delta_{0} \gt P{/tex} , strong field ligand/Weak ligand form high spin complexes whereas strong field form low spin complexes.
solution_html:
1. Aquacyanidobis (ethane-1, 2-diamine) cobalt (III) ion
    2. Tetrachloridoplatinate(II) ion
    3. Tetraamminechloridonitrito-O-chromium (III) ion
  2. The arrangement of ligands in increasing order of field strength is called spectrochemical series /it is an experimentally determined series based on the absorption of light by complexes with different ligands.
    {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt P{/tex}, weak field ligand; {tex}\Delta_{0} \gt P{/tex}, strong field ligand/Weak ligand form high spin complexes whereas strong field form low spin complexes.

explanation: {tex}\left.{Fe}\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}{/tex} - outer orbital (high spin) complex-sp3d {tex}{ }^2{/tex} hybridisation, octahedral, diamagnetic. {tex}\left[{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} - {tex}s p^3{/tex} , (Electrons pair up as {tex}{NH}_3{/tex} is a strong ligand) tetrahedral, paramagnetic.
solution: {tex}\left.{Fe}\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}{/tex} - outer orbital (high spin) complex-sp3d {tex}{ }^2{/tex} hybridisation, octahedral, diamagnetic. {tex}\left[{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} - {tex}s p^3{/tex} , (Electrons pair up as {tex}{NH}_3{/tex} is a strong ligand) tetrahedral, paramagnetic.

  2. {tex}\left[{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} - {tex}s p^3{/tex}, (Electrons pair up as {tex}{NH}_3{/tex} is a strong ligand) tetrahedral, paramagnetic.

1. Using IUPAC norms write the names of the following:
    1. {tex}\left. \mid {Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}\left({NO}_{2}\right)\right] {Cl}{/tex}
    2. {tex}{K}_{3}\left[{Fe}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex}
    3. {tex}\left.\mid \operatorname{Cr}\left({C}_{2} {O}_{4}\right)_{3}\right]^{3}{/tex}
  2. What is crystal field splitting energy? Why low spin tetrahedral complexes are not formed?
question_text: Using IUPAC norms write the names of the following: {tex}\left. \mid {Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}\left({NO}_{2}\right)\right] {Cl}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{3}\left[{Fe}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left.\mid \operatorname{Cr}\left({C}_{2} {O}_{4}\right)_{3}\right]^{3}{/tex} What is crystal field splitting energy? Why low spin tetrahedral complexes are not formed?
explanation: Tetraamminechloridonitrito-N-cobalt (III) chloride Potassium hexacyanidoferrate (III) Trioxalatochromate (III) ion The energy required to split the degenerate d-orbitals into two sets of orbitals./The difference of energy between the two sets of {tex}d{/tex} -orbitals {tex}t_{2} g{/tex} and eg due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry The orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing paring of electrons.
solution: Tetraamminechloridonitrito-N-cobalt (III) chloride Potassium hexacyanidoferrate (III) Trioxalatochromate (III) ion The energy required to split the degenerate d-orbitals into two sets of orbitals./The difference of energy between the two sets of {tex}d{/tex} -orbitals {tex}t_{2} g{/tex} and eg due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry The orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing paring of electrons.
solution_html:
1. Tetraamminechloridonitrito-N-cobalt (III) chloride
    2. Potassium hexacyanidoferrate (III)
    3. Trioxalatochromate (III) ion
    • The energy required to split the degenerate d-orbitals into two sets of orbitals./The difference of energy between the two sets of {tex}d{/tex}-orbitals {tex}t_{2} g{/tex} and eg due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry
    • The orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing paring of electrons.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) of coordination compounds is based on the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. The splitting of the d-orbitals provides different electronic arrangements in strong and weak crystal fields. In tetrahedral coordination entity formation, the d-orbital splitting is smaller as compared to the octahedral entity.
Answer the following questions:

  1. On the basis of CFT, explain why {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3}{/tex} complex is coloured? What happens on heating the complex {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3}{/tex}? Give reason. (2)
    [Atomic no. : {tex}{Ti}=22{/tex}]
    1. What is crystal field splitting energy? (1)

      OR

    2. On the basis of {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} and P (pairing energy), how can you differentiate between a strong field ligand and a weak field ligand? (1)
  3. Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes rarely observed? (1)
question_text: The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) of coordination compounds is based on the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. The splitting of the d-orbitals provides different electronic arrangements in strong and weak crystal fields. In tetrahedral coordination entity formation, the d-orbital splitting is smaller as compared to the octahedral entity. What happens on heating the complex {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3}{/tex} ? Give reason. (2) [Atomic no. : {tex}{Ti}=22{/tex} ] What is crystal field splitting energy? (1) OR On the basis of {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} and P (pairing energy), how can you differentiate between a strong field ligand and a weak field ligand? (1) Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes rarely observed? (1)

(d-d transition) When heated, water is lost therefore crystal field splitting does not occur and it becomes colourless. The energy required to split the degenerate d-orbitals into two sets of orbitals ( {tex}{t}_{2} {~g}{/tex} and {tex}{e}_{{g}}{/tex} ). /The difference of energy between the two sets of d -orbitals {tex}{t}_{2} {~g}{/tex} and eg. due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry. OR {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt P{/tex} , weak filed ligand {tex}\Delta_{o} \gt {P}{/tex} , strong field ligand Because the orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing pairing / Due to low crystal field splitting energy.
solution: Due to presence of one unpaired electron in {tex}t_{2 g}{/tex} which gets excited to {tex}{e}_{g}{/tex} / Due to excitation energy {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{ }^{1} \rightarrow {e}_{{g}}{ }^{1}{/tex} , it gives colour. (d-d transition) When heated, water is lost therefore crystal field splitting does not occur and it becomes colourless. The energy required to split the degenerate d-orbitals into two sets of orbitals ( {tex}{t}_{2} {~g}{/tex} and {tex}{e}_{{g}}{/tex} ). /The difference of energy between the two sets of d -orbitals {tex}{t}_{2} {~g}{/tex} and eg. due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry. OR {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt P{/tex} , weak filed ligand {tex}\Delta_{o} \gt {P}{/tex} , strong field ligand Because the orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing pairing / Due to low crystal field splitting energy.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Werner's coordination theory in 1893 was the first attempt to explain the bonding in coordination complexes. It must be remembered that this theory was put forward before the electron had been discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897, and before the electronic theory of valency. Werner did not have any of the modern instrumental techniques and all his studies were made using simple experimental techniques. Werner was able to explain the nature of bonding in complexes and he concluded that in complexes, the metal shows two different sorts of valency: primary and secondary. Primary valences are normally ionisable whereas secondary valences are non ionisable.
Answer the following questions:

  1. One mole of {tex}{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 4 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} precipitates one mole of AgCl when treated with excess of {tex}{AgNO}_{3}{/tex} solution. Write (i) the structural formula of the complex, and (ii) the secondary valency of Cr. (2)
  2. What is the difference between a complex and a double salt? (1)
    1. Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: (1)
      {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{3} {Cl}_{3}\right], \left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3},{/tex} {tex} \left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{5} {Cl}_{1}\right] {Cl}_{2}{/tex}

      OR

    2. Write two differences between primary and secondary valences in coordination compounds. (1)
question_text: Werner's coordination theory in 1893 was the first attempt to explain the bonding in coordination complexes. It must be remembered that this theory was put forward before the electron had been discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897, and before the electronic theory of valency. Werner did not have any of the modern instrumental techniques and all his studies were made using simple experimental techniques. Werner was able to explain the nature of bonding in complexes and he concluded that in complexes, the metal shows two different sorts of valency: primary and secondary. Primary valences are normally ionisable whereas secondary valences are non ionisable. Answer the following questions: One mole of {tex}{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 4 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} precipitates one mole of AgCl when treated with excess of {tex}{AgNO}_{3}{/tex} solution. 1. Name the type of isomerism exhibited by the following compounds. Also draw their corresponding isomers.
    1. {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]\left[{Cr}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex}
    2. {tex}\left[{Co}({en})_{3}\right]^{3+}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\left({NO}_{2}\right)_{3}\right]{/tex}
  2. Differentiate between weak field and strong field ligands. How does the strength of the ligand influence the spin of the complex?
question_text: Name the type of isomerism exhibited by the following compounds. Also draw their corresponding isomers. {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]\left[{Cr}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[{Co}({en})_{3}\right]^{3+}{/tex} {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\left({NO}_{2}\right)_{3}\right]{/tex} Differentiate between weak field and strong field ligands. How does the strength of the ligand influence the spin of the complex?
explanation: Coordination Isomerism/ {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex} Optical Isomerism/ Geometrical isomerism/ Weak field ligands produce weak field and leads to small splitting of d-orbitals whereas strong field ligands produce strong field leading to large splitting of d-orbitals. Strong field ligands cause pairing of electrons/a smaller number of unpaired electrons hence produces low spin complexes and weak field ligands causes no pairing of electrons/a greater number of unpaired electrons hence produces high spin complexes.
solution: Coordination Isomerism/ {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex} Optical Isomerism/ Geometrical isomerism/ Weak field ligands produce weak field and leads to small splitting of d-orbitals whereas strong field ligands produce strong field leading to large splitting of d-orbitals. Strong field ligands cause pairing of electrons/a smaller number of unpaired electrons hence produces low spin complexes and weak field ligands causes no pairing of electrons/a greater number of unpaired electrons hence produces high spin complexes.
solution_html:
1. Coordination Isomerism/{tex}\left[{Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]{/tex}
    2. Optical Isomerism/
    3. Geometrical isomerism/
  2. Weak field ligands produce weak field and leads to small splitting of d-orbitals whereas strong field ligands produce strong field leading to large splitting of d-orbitals.
    Strong field ligands cause pairing of electrons/a smaller number of unpaired electrons hence produces low spin complexes and weak field ligands causes no pairing of electrons/a greater number of unpaired electrons hence produces high spin complexes.

question_text: Give reasons: {tex}\left[{Ni}({CO})_{4}\right]{/tex} is diamagnetic whereas {tex}\left[{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}{/tex} is paramagnetic. [Atomic number: {tex}{Ni}=28{/tex} ] {tex}{CO}{/tex} is a stronger complexing agent than {tex}{NH}_{3}{/tex} . The trans isomer of complex {tex}\left[{Co}({en})_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}{/tex} is optically inactive. Using Crystal Field theory, write the number of unpaired electrons in octahedral complexes of {tex}{Fe}^{3+}{/tex} in the presence of: Strong field ligand Weak field ligand [Atomic number : {tex}{Fe}=26{/tex} ]
explanation: CO being a strong field ligand, causes pairing of electrons therefore, there is no unpaired electron. Whereas {tex}{Cl}^{-}{/tex} is a weak field ligand, does not cause pairing, therefore presence of unpaired electrons. CO can form both sigma ( {tex}\sigma{/tex} ) and pi ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) bond with central metal atom/Metal to ligand bonding creates synergic effect between CO and the Metal. Mirror images are superimposable/ Presence of plane of symmetry. {tex}\Delta_0>{P}{/tex} , causes pairing of electrons, therefore 1 unpaired electron {tex}\Delta_0<{P}{/tex} , No pairing of electrons therefore 5 unpaired electrons
solution: CO being a strong field ligand, causes pairing of electrons therefore, there is no unpaired electron. Whereas {tex}{Cl}^{-}{/tex} is a weak field ligand, does not cause pairing, therefore presence of unpaired electrons. CO can form both sigma ( {tex}\sigma{/tex} ) and pi ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) bond with central metal atom/Metal to ligand bonding creates synergic effect between CO and the Metal. Mirror images are superimposable/ Presence of plane of symmetry. {tex}\Delta_0>{P}{/tex} , causes pairing of electrons, therefore 1 unpaired electron {tex}\Delta_0<{P}{/tex} , No pairing of electrons therefore 5 unpaired electrons
solution_html:
1. CO being a strong field ligand, causes pairing of electrons therefore, there is no unpaired electron.
      Whereas {tex}{Cl}^{-}{/tex}is a weak field ligand, does not cause pairing, therefore presence of unpaired electrons.
    2. CO can form both sigma ({tex}\sigma{/tex}) and pi ({tex}\pi{/tex}) bond with central metal atom/Metal to ligand bonding creates synergic effect between CO and the Metal.
    3. Mirror images are superimposable/ Presence of plane of symmetry.
    1. {tex}\Delta_0>{P}{/tex}, causes pairing of electrons, therefore 1 unpaired electron
    2. {tex}\Delta_0<{P}{/tex}, No pairing of electrons therefore 5 unpaired electrons

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Differentiate between:

  1. Double salt and Complex compound
  2. Didentate ligand and Ambidentate ligand
question_text: Differentiate between: Double salt and Complex compound Didentate ligand and Ambidentate ligand
explanation: Double salts dissociate into simple ions while complex compounds do not dissociate completely into ions when dissolved in water. When a ligand binds through two donor atoms is called a didentate ligand while a unidentate ligand which has two different donor atoms and either of the two ligates in the complex is called ambidentate ligand.
solution: Double salts dissociate into simple ions while complex compounds do not dissociate completely into ions when dissolved in water. When a ligand binds through two donor atoms is called a didentate ligand while a unidentate ligand which has two different donor atoms and either of the two ligates in the complex is called ambidentate ligand.
solution_html:
1. Double salts dissociate into simple ions while complex compounds do not dissociate completely into ions when dissolved in water.
  2. When a ligand binds through two donor atoms is called a didentate ligand while a unidentate ligand which has two different donor atoms and either of the two ligates in the complex is called ambidentate ligand.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
One of the most distinctive properties of transition metal complexes is their wide range of colours. This means that some of the visible spectrum is being removed from white light as it passes through the sample, so the light that emerges is no longer white. The colour of the complex is complementary to that which is absorbed. The complementary colour is the colour generated from the wavelength left over. If green light is absorbed by the complex, it appears red. Table given below, gives the relationship between different wavelengths of the light absorbed and the colour observed.

Coordination Entity Wavelength of light absorbed (nm) Colour of light absorbed Colour of coordination entity
{tex}\left[{CoCl}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{5}\right]^{2+}{/tex} 535 Yellow Violet
{tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{5}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)\right]^{3+}{/tex} 500 Blue-green Red
{tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} 475 Blue Yellow-orange
{tex}\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}{/tex} 310 Ultra-Violet (U.V.) Pale Yellow
{tex}\left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex} 600 Red Blue
{tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} 498 Blue-green Violet
  1. What is spectrochemical series? Write the relationship between {tex}\Delta_{{t}}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex}. (2)
    1. Arrange {tex}\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}{/tex}, {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} and {tex}\left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex} in increasing order of wavelength of light absorbed. (1)

      OR

    2. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} ion if {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt {P}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Define crystal field splitting energy. (1)

Pale Yellow {tex}\left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex} 600 Red Blue {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} 498 Blue-green Violet What is spectrochemical series? explanation: Spectrochemical series: Arrangement of ligands in the increasing order of their field strength. {tex}\Delta_{ t}=(\overline{4} / 9) \Delta_{0}{/tex} {tex}\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]^{3-} \lt \left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} \lt \left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex} OR {tex}t_{2 g}^{3} e_{g}^{2}{/tex} The energy required to separate the degenerate d-orbitals into {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{/tex} and {tex}{e}_{{g}}{/tex} sets, when ligands approach the central metal atom/ion.
solution: Spectrochemical series: Arrangement of ligands in the increasing order of their field strength. {tex}\Delta_{ t}=(\overline{4} / 9) \Delta_{0}{/tex} {tex}\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]^{3-} \lt \left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} \lt \left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex} OR {tex}t_{2 g}^{3} e_{g}^{2}{/tex} The energy required to separate the degenerate d-orbitals into {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{/tex} and {tex}{e}_{{g}}{/tex} sets, when ligands approach the central metal atom/ion.

    1. {tex}\left[{Co}({CN})_{6}\right]^{3-} \lt \left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} \lt \left[{Cu}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+}{/tex}

question_html: Why is geometrical isomerism not possible in tetrahedral complexes having two different types of unidentate ligands coordinated with the central metal ion?
question_text: Why is geometrical isomerism not possible in tetrahedral complexes having two different types of unidentate ligands coordinated with the central metal ion?
explanation: Tetrahedral complexes do not show geometrical isomerism(a type of stereoisomerism) because the relative positions of the unidentate ligands attached to the central metal atom are the same with respect to each other.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds whereas "The Crystal Field Theory" for coordination compounds is based on the effect of different crystal fields (provided by ligands taken as point charges), on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. The splitting of the d-orbitals provides different electronic arrangements in strong and weak crystal fields. The crystal field theory attributes the colour of the coordination compounds to d-d transition of the electron. Coordination compounds find extensive applications in metallurgical processes, analytical and medicinal chemistry.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What is crystal field splitting energy? (1)
  2. Give reason for the violet colour of the complex [Ti(H2O)6]3+ on the basis of crystal field theory. (1)
  3. [Cr(NH3)6]3+ is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN)4]2- is diamagnetic. Explain why. [Atomic No.: Cr = 24, Ni = 28] (2)

    OR

    Explain why [Fe(CN)6]3- is an inner orbital complex, whereas [Fe(H2O)6]3+ is an outer orbital complex. (2)
    [Atomic No.: Fe = 26]
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds whereas "The Crystal Field Theory" for coordination compounds is based on the effect of different crystal fields (provided by ligands taken as point charges), on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. The splitting of the d-orbitals provides different electronic arrangements in strong and weak crystal fields. The crystal field theory attributes the colour of the coordination compounds to d-d transition of the electron. Coordination compounds find extensive applications in metallurgical processes, analytical and medicinal chemistry. Answer the following questions: What is crystal field splitting energy? (1) Give reason for the violet colour of the complex [Ti(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ on the basis of crystal field theory. (1) [Cr(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- is diamagnetic. Explain why. [Atomic No.: Cr = 24, Ni = 28] (2) OR Explain why [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- is an inner orbital complex, whereas [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ is an outer orbital complex. (2) [Atomic No.: Fe = 26]
explanation: The energy used in the splitting of degenerate d- orbitals due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry is called Crystal Field Splitting Energy. Ti 3+ = 3d 1 i.e. t 2g 1 e g o Due to d-d transition. Cr 3+ = 3d 3 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} Due to stable t 2g 3 configuration, hence paramagnetic. Ni 2+ = 3d 8 CN - being strong field ligand pair up the electrons and hence diamagnetic. OR CN - being a strong ligand leads to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- leading to d 2 sp 3 hybridization.H 2 O being a weak ligand does not lead to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ leading to sp 3 d 2 hybridization. / In [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- , (n-1)d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (d 2 sp 3 ). Hence inner orbital complex whereas in [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ n d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (sp 3 d 2 ).
solution: The energy used in the splitting of degenerate d- orbitals due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry is called Crystal Field Splitting Energy. Ti 3+ = 3d 1 i.e. t 2g 1 e g o Due to d-d transition. Cr 3+ = 3d 3 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} Due to stable t 2g 3 configuration, hence paramagnetic. Ni 2+ = 3d 8 CN - being strong field ligand pair up the electrons and hence diamagnetic. OR CN - being a strong ligand leads to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- leading to d 2 sp 3 hybridization.H 2 O being a weak ligand does not lead to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ leading to sp 3 d 2 hybridization. / In [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- , (n-1)d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (d 2 sp 3 ). Hence inner orbital complex whereas in [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ n d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (sp 3 d 2 ).
solution_html:
1. The energy used in the splitting of degenerate d- orbitals due to the presence of ligands in a definite geometry is called Crystal Field Splitting Energy.
  2. Ti3+ = 3d1 i.e. t2g 1ego Due to d-d transition.
  3. Cr3+ = 3d3 
    {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex}    

    Due to stable t2g3 configuration, hence paramagnetic.
    Ni2+ = 3d8 
    CN- being strong field ligand pair up the electrons and hence diamagnetic.
    OR
    CN- being a strong ligand leads to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(CN)6]3- leading to d2sp3 hybridization.H2O being a weak ligand does not lead to the pairing of electrons in [Fe(H2O)6]3+ leading to sp3d2 hybridization. / In [Fe(CN)6]3-, (n-1)d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (d2sp3). Hence inner orbital complex whereas in [Fe(H2O)6]3+ n d orbitals of central metal ion are used in hybridization (sp3d2).

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The nature of bonding, structure of the coordination compound can be explained to some extent by valence bond theory. The central metal atom/ion makes available a number of vacant orbitals equal to its coordination number. The appropriate atomic orbitals (s, p and d) of the metal hybridise to give a set of equivalent orbitals of definite geometry such as square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral and so on. A strong covalent bond is formed only when the orbitals overlap to the maximum extent. The d-orbitals involved in the hybridisation may be either inner d-orbitals i.e. ({tex}{n}-1{/tex}) d or outer d-orbitals i.e. nd. The complexes formed are called inner orbital complex (low spin complex) and outer orbital complex (high spin complex) respectively. Further, the complexes can be paramagnetic or diamagnetic in nature. The drawbacks of this theory are that this involves number of assumptions and also does not explain the colour of the complex.
Answer the following questions:

  1. Predict whether {tex}\left[{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}{/tex} is diamagnetic or paramagnetic and why? (1)
    [Atomic number : {tex}{Co}=27{/tex}]
  2. What is the coordination number of Co in {tex}\left[{Co}({en})_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}{/tex}? (1)
    1. Write the IUPAC name of the given complex:
      {tex}\left[{Pt}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex}
    2. Explain {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} is an inner orbital or outer orbital complex. (2)

      OR

      Using valence bond theory, deduce the shape and hybridisation of {tex}\left[{Ni}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} [Atomic number of {tex}{Ni}=28{/tex}] (2)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The oxidation number of the central atom in a complex is defined as the charge it would carry if all the ligands are removed along with the electron pairs that are shared with the central atom. Similarly the charge on the complex is the sum of the charges of the constituent parts i.e. the sum of the charges on the central metal ion and its surrounding ligands. Based on this, the complex is called neutral if the sum of the charges of the constituents is equal to zero. However, for an anion or cationic complex, the sum of the charges of the constituents is equal to the charge on the coordination sphere.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the secondary valence of Co in {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}{/tex}? (1)
  2. What type of isomerism is shown by the complex {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl} l_{3}{/tex} and {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{5} {Cl}\right] {Cl}_{2} \cdot {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}? (1)
    1. Write the electronic configuration of {tex}d^{4}{/tex} ion on the basis of crystal field theory when (2)
      1. {tex}\Delta_{0} \lt {P}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\Delta_{0} \gt {P}{/tex}

        OR

    2. Find the oxidation state and coordination number of the central metal ion in {tex}\left[{Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)({CN})({en})_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex} (2)

question_html:
Assertion (A): Low spin tetrahedral complexes are rarely observed.
Reason (R): Crystal field splitting energy is less than pairing energy for tetrahedral complexes.
question_text: Assertion (A): Low spin tetrahedral complexes are rarely observed. Reason (R): Crystal field splitting energy is less than pairing energy for tetrahedral complexes.

options.text: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
options.html: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
options.explanation: In the tetrahedral complex, the d orbital is splitting to small as compared to the octahedral. Hence the orbital splitting energies are not enough to force paring. As a result, the low spin configuration is rarely observed in tetrahedral complexes.

question_text: On the basis of crystal field theory write the electronic configuration for d 5 ion with a weak ligand for which {tex}\Delta_0<\mathrm{P}{/tex} . Explain [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- is an inner orbital complex whereas [FeF 6 ] 3- is an outer orbital complex. [Atomic number : Fe = 26]
explanation: For weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} i.e., pairing will not occur for d 5 ion. [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- Fe = 3 oxidation state i.e., electronic configuration = 3d 5 CN - = strong field ligand i.e., {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} pairing will take place Electronic configuration of Fe 3+ = 3d 5 inner orbit complex. [FeF 6 ] 3- Fe = +3 oxidation state F - = Weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Pairing will not take place. sp 3 d 2 = Outer orbital complex.
solution: For weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} i.e., pairing will not occur for d 5 ion. [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- Fe = 3 oxidation state i.e., electronic configuration = 3d 5 CN - = strong field ligand i.e., {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} pairing will take place Electronic configuration of Fe 3+ = 3d 5 inner orbit complex. [FeF 6 ] 3- Fe = +3 oxidation state F - = Weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Pairing will not take place. sp 3 d 2 = Outer orbital complex.
solution_html:
1. For weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} i.e., pairing will not occur for d5 ion.
  2. [Fe(CN)6]3-
    Fe = 3 oxidation state
    i.e., electronic configuration = 3d5
    CN- = strong field ligand
    i.e., {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} pairing will take place
    Electronic configuration of Fe3+ = 3d5inner orbit complex.

    [FeF6]3-
    Fe = +3 oxidation state
    F- = Weak field ligand {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Pairing will not take place.

    sp3d2 = Outer orbital complex.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 with a strong field ligand for which {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex}.
  2. A solution of [Ni(H2O)6]2+ is green but a solution of [Ni(CO)4] is colourless. Explain.
    [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
question_text: On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d 4 with a strong field ligand for which {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} . A solution of [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ is green but a solution of [Ni(CO) 4 ] is colourless. Explain. [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
explanation: If {tex}\Delta_0>\mathrm{P}{/tex} , then on the basis of crystal field theory the electronic configuration of the d 4 ion will be {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 g}^4 \mathrm{e}_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation. H 2 O is a weak field ligand in [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ . As a result, Ni 2+ has unpaired electrons. In this compound, d-electrons from the lower energy level can be stimulated to the higher energy level, implying that a d-d transition is possible. As a result, [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ appears green, whereas [Ni(CO) 4 ] is colourless due to the absence of an unpaired electron in the complex. CO is a strong field ligand that produces electron pairing.
solution: If {tex}\Delta_0>\mathrm{P}{/tex} , then on the basis of crystal field theory the electronic configuration of the d 4 ion will be {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 g}^4 \mathrm{e}_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation. H 2 O is a weak field ligand in [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ . As a result, Ni 2+ has unpaired electrons. In this compound, d-electrons from the lower energy level can be stimulated to the higher energy level, implying that a d-d transition is possible. As a result, [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ appears green, whereas [Ni(CO) 4 ] is colourless due to the absence of an unpaired electron in the complex. CO is a strong field ligand that produces electron pairing.
solution_html:
1. If {tex}\Delta_0>\mathrm{P}{/tex}, then on the basis of crystal field theory the electronic configuration of the d4 ion will be {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 g}^4 \mathrm{e}_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation.
  2. H2O is a weak field ligand in [Ni(H2O)6]2+. As a result, Ni2+ has unpaired electrons. In this compound, d-electrons from the lower energy level can be stimulated to the higher energy level, implying that a d-d transition is possible. As a result, [Ni(H2O)6]2+ appears green, whereas [Ni(CO)4] is colourless due to the absence of an unpaired electron in the complex. CO is a strong field ligand that produces electron pairing.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. On the basis of crystal field theory write the electronic configuration for d5 ion with a strong field ligand for which {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex}.
  2. [Ni(CO)4] has tetrahedral geometry while [Ni(CN)4]2- has square planar yet both exhibit dimagnetism. Explain.
    [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
question_text: On the basis of crystal field theory write the electronic configuration for d 5 ion with a strong field ligand for which {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} . [Ni(CO) 4 ] has tetrahedral geometry while [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- has square planar yet both exhibit dimagnetism. Explain. [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
explanation: If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} , it becomes more energetically favourable for the fourth electron to occupy a t 2g orbital with electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^5 e_g^0{/tex} . Ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands and form low-spin complexes. In [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- , nickel is in a + 2 oxidation state and the ion has the electronic configuration 3d 8 . The hybridisation scheme is shown in the diagram. Whereas in [Ni(CO) 4 ], Ni has + 2 oxidation state and shows sp 2 hybridisation due to which its geometry is tetrahedral hence diamagnetic.
solution: If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} , it becomes more energetically favourable for the fourth electron to occupy a t 2g orbital with electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^5 e_g^0{/tex} . Ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands and form low-spin complexes. In [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- , nickel is in a + 2 oxidation state and the ion has the electronic configuration 3d 8 . The hybridisation scheme is shown in the diagram. Whereas in [Ni(CO) 4 ], Ni has + 2 oxidation state and shows sp 2 hybridisation due to which its geometry is tetrahedral hence diamagnetic.
solution_html:
1. If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex}, it becomes more energetically favourable for the fourth electron to occupy a t2g orbital with electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^5 e_g^0{/tex}. Ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands and form low-spin complexes.
  2. In [Ni(CN)4]2-, nickel is in a + 2 oxidation state and the ion has the electronic configuration 3d8. The hybridisation scheme is shown in the diagram.

    Whereas in [Ni(CO)4], Ni has + 2 oxidation state and shows sp2 hybridisation due to which its geometry is tetrahedral hence diamagnetic.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Coordination compounds are widely present in the minerals, plant and animal worlds and are known to play many important functions in the area of analytical chemistry, metallurgy, biological systems and medicine. Alfred Werner's theory postulated the use of two types of linkages (primary and secondary), by a metal atom/ion in a coordination compound. He predicted the geometrical shapes of a large number of coordination entities using the property of isomerism. The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds. It, however, fails to describe the optical properties of these compounds. The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) explains the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion.


When a coordination compound NiCl2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H2O is mixed with AgNO3 solution, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write the structural formula of the complex and secondary valency for Nickel ion.

He predicted the geometrical shapes of a large number of coordination entities using the property of isomerism. The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds. It, however, fails to describe the optical properties of these compounds. The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) explains the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. When a coordination compound NiCl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H 2 O is mixed with AgNO 3 solution, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write the structural formula of the complex and secondary valency for Nickel ion.
explanation: [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 2 , 6
solution: [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 2 , 6

He predicted the geometrical shapes of a large number of coordination entities using the property of isomerism. The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds. It, however, fails to describe the optical properties of these compounds. The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) explains the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion.


Give reasons:

  1. Low spin tetrahedral complexes are not formed.
  2. [Co(NH3)6]3+ is an inner orbital complex whereas [Ni(NH3)6]2+ is an outer orbital complex.
    [Atomic number : Co = 27, Ni = 28]
question_text: Coordination compounds are widely present in the minerals, plant and animal worlds and are known to play many important functions in the area of analytical chemistry, metallurgy, biological systems and medicine. Alfred Werner's theory postulated the use of two types of linkages (primary and secondary), by a metal atom/ion in a coordination compound. He predicted the geometrical shapes of a large number of coordination entities using the property of isomerism. The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) explains the formation, magnetic behaviour and geometrical shapes of coordination compounds. It, however, fails to describe the optical properties of these compounds. The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) explains the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. Give reasons: Low spin tetrahedral complexes are not formed. [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is an inner orbital complex whereas [Ni(NH 3 ) 6 ] 2+ is an outer orbital complex. [Atomic number : Co = 27, Ni = 28]
explanation: Because {tex}\Delta_t {/tex} is not sufficient for the pairing of electrons/Crystal field splitting energy (CFSE) is not sufficient for pairing of electrons. NH 3 being a strong field ligand can pair up the electrons to form d 2 sp 3 but cannot pair up in Ni 2+ as two vacant d-orbitals are not available. {tex}\therefore{/tex} sp 3 d 2 is formed.
solution: Because {tex}\Delta_t {/tex} is not sufficient for the pairing of electrons/Crystal field splitting energy (CFSE) is not sufficient for pairing of electrons. NH 3 being a strong field ligand can pair up the electrons to form d 2 sp 3 but cannot pair up in Ni 2+ as two vacant d-orbitals are not available. {tex}\therefore{/tex} sp 3 d 2 is formed.
solution_html:
1. Because {tex}\Delta_t {/tex} is not sufficient for the pairing of electrons/Crystal field splitting energy (CFSE) is not sufficient for pairing of electrons.
  2. NH3 being a strong field ligand can pair up the electrons to form d2sp3 but cannot pair up in Ni2+ as two vacant d-orbitals are not available.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} sp3d2 is formed.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In coordination compounds, metals show two types of linkages, primary and secondary. Primary valencies are ionisable and are satisfied by negatively charged ions. Secondary valencies are non-ionisable and are satisfied by neutral or negative ions having lone pair of electrons. Primary valencies are non-directional while secondary valencies decide the shape of the complexes.

question_text: Write the hybridisation and magnetic character of the following complexes: [NiCl 4 ] 2- [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [FeF 6 ] 3- [Atomic number: Ni = 28, Co = 27, Fe = 26]
explanation: The central metal Ni undergoes sp 3 hybridisation. It is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired e - in d orbitals. In [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ the oxidation state of cobalt is +3. Ammonia is a strong field ligand so it pair up 4 unpaired electron and free up 2 3-d orbitals. These 3-d orbitals are involved in hybridisation with one 4S and three 4P orbitals forming an inner orbital complex, so hybridisation of [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is d 2 sp 3 Since it has no unpaired electrons, [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is diamagnetic. The hybridization of the central iron atom in [FeF 6 ] 3 - is sp 3 d 2 . The complex is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the 3d orbitals of the iron atom.
solution: The central metal Ni undergoes sp 3 hybridisation. It is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired e - in d orbitals. In [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ the oxidation state of cobalt is +3. Ammonia is a strong field ligand so it pair up 4 unpaired electron and free up 2 3-d orbitals. These 3-d orbitals are involved in hybridisation with one 4S and three 4P orbitals forming an inner orbital complex, so hybridisation of [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is d 2 sp 3 Since it has no unpaired electrons, [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is diamagnetic. The hybridization of the central iron atom in [FeF 6 ] 3 - is sp 3 d 2 . The complex is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the 3d orbitals of the iron atom.
solution_html:
1. The central metal Ni undergoes sp3 hybridisation. It is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired e- in d orbitals.
  2. In [Co(NH3)6]3+ the oxidation state of cobalt is +3. Ammonia is a strong field ligand so it pair up 4 unpaired electron and free up 2 3-d orbitals. These 3-d orbitals are involved in hybridisation with one 4S and three 4P orbitals forming an inner orbital complex, so hybridisation of [Co(NH3)6]3+ is d2 sp3
    Since it has no unpaired electrons, [Co(NH3)6]3+ is diamagnetic.
  3. The hybridization of the central iron atom in [FeF6]3- is sp3 d2.
    The complex is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the 3d orbitals of the iron atom.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Co(NH3)4(H2O)Cl]Cl2
  2. What is the difference between an Ambidentate ligand and a Bidentate ligand?
  3. Out of [Fe(NH3)6]3+ and [Fe(C2O4)3]3-, which complex is more stable and why?
question_text: Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Co(NH 3 ) 4 (H 2 O)Cl]Cl 2 What is the difference between an Ambidentate ligand and a Bidentate ligand? Out of [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ and [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- , which complex is more stable and why?
explanation: The IUPAC name of this compound is Tetraammineaquachloro cobalt(III) chloride. Ambidentate ligands can bond to a central metal atom through two different donor atoms in separate ligand molecules (e.g., NO 2- , which can bind through N or O). Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms within the same ligand molecule, allowing them to form two coordination bonds simultaneously with the metal center (e.g., ethylenediamine, which has two amino groups). [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is generally more stable than [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- . Ammonia is a stronger ligand than oxalate, and the coordination number is the same for both complexes. Furthermore, the chelation effect of oxalate in [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- is not strong enough to overcome the inherent strength of ammonia as a ligand. Therefore, [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is typically the more stable complex.
solution: The IUPAC name of this compound is Tetraammineaquachloro cobalt(III) chloride. Ambidentate ligands can bond to a central metal atom through two different donor atoms in separate ligand molecules (e.g., NO 2- , which can bind through N or O). Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms within the same ligand molecule, allowing them to form two coordination bonds simultaneously with the metal center (e.g., ethylenediamine, which has two amino groups). [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is generally more stable than [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- . Ammonia is a stronger ligand than oxalate, and the coordination number is the same for both complexes. Furthermore, the chelation effect of oxalate in [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- is not strong enough to overcome the inherent strength of ammonia as a ligand. Therefore, [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is typically the more stable complex.
solution_html:
1.  The IUPAC name of this compound is Tetraammineaquachloro cobalt(III) chloride.
  2. Ambidentate ligands can bond to a central metal atom through two different donor atoms in separate ligand molecules (e.g., NO2-, which can bind through N or O). Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms within the same ligand molecule, allowing them to form two coordination bonds simultaneously with the metal center (e.g., ethylenediamine, which has two amino groups).
  3. [Fe(NH3)6]3+ is generally more stable than [Fe(C2O4)3]3-. Ammonia is a stronger ligand than oxalate, and the coordination number is the same for both complexes. Furthermore, the chelation effect of oxalate in [Fe(C2O4)3]3- is not strong enough to overcome the inherent strength of ammonia as a ligand. Therefore, [Fe(NH3)6]3+ is typically the more stable complex.

question_text: Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: K 2 [PdCl 4 ] Using crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d 5 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P. What are Homoleptic complexes?
explanation: IUPAC name of K 2 [PdCl 4 ] is Potassium tetrachloroethylene palladate (II). If {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} > P, the electronic configuration of d 5 ion will be {tex}t_{2 g}^5 \ e_g^0{/tex} as it is associated with strong field and low spin situation. Thus, no electron will enter into {tex}e_g{/tex} orbital. The complexes in which metal ion is bound to only one type of ligands are called homoleptic complexes. e.g. In [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ , only one type of ligand, NH 3 surrounds the Co 3+ ion.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why chelate complexes are more stable than complexes with unidentate ligands?
  2. What is spectrochemical series? What is the difference between a weak field ligand and a strong field ligand?
question_text: Why chelate complexes are more stable than complexes with unidentate ligands? What is spectrochemical series ? What is the difference between a weak field ligand and a strong field ligand?
explanation: Chelate complexes are more stable than unidentate ligands because they form a ring with the central metal ion and are held together by a strong force of attraction, making them less likely to dissolve. Unidentate ligands, on the other hand, are connected to the core metal at a single location, resulting in less force of attraction and a greater likelihood of dissociation. A spectrochemical series is the arrangement of common ligands in the increasing order of their crystal-field splitting energy (CFSE) values. The ligands present on the R.H.S of the series are strong field ligands while that on the L.H.S are weak field ligands. Also, strong field ligands cause higher splitting in the d orbitals than weak field ligands. question_text: Using IUPAC norms write the formulas for the following: Pentaamminenitrito-N-Cobalt (III) Tetrahydroxidozincate (II) What is crystal field splitting energy?
explanation: Chemical formula of Pentaamminenitrito-N-cobalt(III) is [Co(NO 2 )(NH 3 ) 5 ] 2+ . Chemical formula of Tetrabromidocuprate(II) is [Cu(Br 4 )] 2- . The crystal field stabilisation energy (CFSE) is the gain in the energy achieved by preferential filling up of orbitals by electrons. It is usually less than or equal to o. When it is equal to o, the complex is unstable. The magnitude of CFSE depends on the number and nature of ligands and the geometry of the complex.
solution: Chemical formula of Pentaamminenitrito-N-cobalt(III) is [Co(NO 2 )(NH 3 ) 5 ] 2+ . Chemical formula of Tetrabromidocuprate(II) is [Cu(Br 4 )] 2- . question_text: Write the IUPAC name and hybridisation of the complex [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3– . (Given: Atomic number of Fe = 26) What is the difference between an ambidentate ligand and a chelating ligand?
explanation: IUPAC name of [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3 is hexacyanoferrate (III) ion. hybridisation - d 2 sp 3 Ambidentate ligand can bond through different atoms to form different coordination compounds. e.g. {tex}NO_2 ^{-}{/tex} and SCN - ion. {tex}NO_2 ^{-}{/tex} can bind to the central atom or ion at either the nitrogen atom or one of the oxygen atom. Similarly SCN - can coordinate through the sulphur and nitrogen atom. Chelating ligand: If the ligands with two or more electron donor groups positioned in such a way that they form a five or six-membered ring with central metal ion are called chelating ligands. e.g. ethane - 1, 2-diamine (en).

question_html:

  2. What is meant by Chelate effect? Give an example.
question_text: Give the IUPAC name and electronic configuration of central metal atom in terms of t 2g and e g of K 4 [Mn(CN) 6 ]. What is meant by Chelate effect ? Give an example.
explanation: The IUPAC name of K 4 [Mn(CN) 6 ] is Potassium hexacyanomanganate (II) Electronic configuration : {tex} {d^{5 + }}:{t_{2g}}^5{/tex} Mn: {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{5} \mathrm{eg}^{0}{/tex} When a ligand attaches to the metal ion in a manner that forms a ring, then the metal - ligand association is found to be more stable. In other words, we can say that complexes containing chelate rings are more stable than complexes without rings. This is known as the chelate effect. For example: Ni 2+ (aq) + 6NH 3 (aq) {tex} \leftrightarrow {/tex} [Ni(NH 3 ) 6 ] 2+ (aq) [log {tex}\beta{/tex} =7.99]
solution: The IUPAC name of K 4 [Mn(CN) 6 ] is Potassium hexacyanomanganate (II) Electronic configuration : {tex} {d^{5 + }}:{t_{2g}}^5{/tex} Mn: {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{5} \mathrm{eg}^{0}{/tex} When a ligand attaches to the metal ion in a manner that forms a ring, then the metal - ligand association is found to be more stable. In other words, we can say that complexes containing chelate rings are more stable than complexes without rings. This is known as the chelate effect. For example: Ni 2+ (aq) + 6NH 3 (aq) {tex} \leftrightarrow {/tex} [Ni(NH 3 ) 6 ] 2+ (aq) [log {tex}\beta{/tex} =7.99]
solution_html:
1. The IUPAC name of K4[Mn(CN)6] is
    Potassium hexacyanomanganate (II)
    Electronic configuration : {tex} {d^{5 + }}:{t_{2g}}^5{/tex} 
    Mn: {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{5} \mathrm{eg}^{0}{/tex} 
  2. When a ligand attaches to the metal ion in a manner that forms a ring, then the metal - ligand association is found to be more stable. In other words, we can say that complexes containing chelate rings are more stable than complexes without rings. This is known as the chelate effect. For example:
    Ni2+ (aq) + 6NH3 (aq) {tex} \leftrightarrow {/tex} [Ni(NH3)6]2+ (aq) [log {tex}\beta{/tex} =7.99]

question_html:
Write the hybridisation and magnetic characters of the following complexes :

  1. [Fe(CN)6]4-
  2. [CoF6]3-
  3. [Ni(CO)4]
    [Atomic number: Fe = 26, Co = 27, Ni = 28]
question_text: Write the hybridisation and magnetic characters of the following complexes : [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4- [CoF 6 ] 3- [Ni(CO) 4 ] [Atomic number: Fe = 26, Co = 27, Ni = 28]
explanation: {tex}[ \left.{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex} Metal oxidation state: Fe is +2 (since {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex} is -1 and there are 6 ligands, total charge on complex = -4) Electronic configuration of {tex}{Fe}^{2+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex} Nature of ligand: {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex} is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex} Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (All electrons paired due to strong field ligand) {tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex} Metal oxidation state: Co is +3 ( {tex}{F}^{-}{/tex} is -1 and complex charge is -3 ) Electronic configuration of {tex}{Co}^{3+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex} Nature of ligand: {tex}F^{-}{/tex} is a weak field ligand (does not cause pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} (since it's an outer orbital complex due to weak field ligand) Magnetic character: Paramagnetic (4 unpaired electrons remain in the {tex}3 d{/tex} orbitals) {tex}[\left.{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex} Metal oxidation state: Ni is O (neutral complex) Electronic configuration of Ni: {tex}[A r] 3 d^8 4 s^2{/tex} Nature of ligand: CO is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}s p^3{/tex} (tetrahedral geometry with hybridization involving one {tex}s{/tex} and three p orbitals) Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (all electrons are paired) Complex Metal Oxidation State Ligand Field Strength Hybridization Magnetic Nature {tex}\left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-}{/tex} +2 Strong {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic {tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex} +3 Weak {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} Paramagnetic {tex}\left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex} 0 Strong {tex}s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic
solution: {tex}[ \left.{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex} Metal oxidation state: Fe is +2 (since {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex} is -1 and there are 6 ligands, total charge on complex = -4) Electronic configuration of {tex}{Fe}^{2+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex} Nature of ligand: {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex} is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex} Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (All electrons paired due to strong field ligand) {tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex} Metal oxidation state: Co is +3 ( {tex}{F}^{-}{/tex} is -1 and complex charge is -3 ) Electronic configuration of {tex}{Co}^{3+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex} Nature of ligand: {tex}F^{-}{/tex} is a weak field ligand (does not cause pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} (since it's an outer orbital complex due to weak field ligand) Magnetic character: Paramagnetic (4 unpaired electrons remain in the {tex}3 d{/tex} orbitals) {tex}[\left.{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex} Metal oxidation state: Ni is O (neutral complex) Electronic configuration of Ni: {tex}[A r] 3 d^8 4 s^2{/tex} Nature of ligand: CO is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons) Hybridization: {tex}s p^3{/tex} (tetrahedral geometry with hybridization involving one {tex}s{/tex} and three p orbitals) Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (all electrons are paired) Complex Metal Oxidation State Ligand Field Strength Hybridization Magnetic Nature {tex}\left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-}{/tex} +2 Strong {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic {tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex} +3 Weak {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} Paramagnetic {tex}\left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex} 0 Strong {tex}s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic
solution_html:
{tex}[ \left.{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex}

Metal oxidation state: Fe is +2 (since {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex}is -1 and there are 6 ligands, total charge on complex = -4)
Electronic configuration of {tex}{Fe}^{2+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex}
Nature of ligand: {tex}{CN}^{-}{/tex}is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons)
Hybridization: {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex}
Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (All electrons paired due to strong field ligand)

{tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex}
Metal oxidation state: Co is +3 ( {tex}{F}^{-}{/tex}is -1 and complex charge is -3 )
Electronic configuration of {tex}{Co}^{3+}:[A r] 3 d^6 4 s^0 4 p^0{/tex}
Nature of ligand: {tex}F^{-}{/tex}is a weak field ligand (does not cause pairing of electrons)
Hybridization: {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} (since it's an outer orbital complex due to weak field ligand)
Magnetic character: Paramagnetic (4 unpaired electrons remain in the {tex}3 d{/tex} orbitals)

{tex}[\left.{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex}
Metal oxidation state: Ni is O (neutral complex)
Electronic configuration of Ni: {tex}[A r] 3 d^8 4 s^2{/tex}
Nature of ligand: CO is a strong field ligand (causes pairing of electrons)
Hybridization: {tex}s p^3{/tex} (tetrahedral geometry with hybridization involving one {tex}s{/tex} and three p orbitals)

Magnetic character: Diamagnetic (all electrons are paired)

Complex Metal Oxidation State Ligand Field Strength Hybridization Magnetic Nature
{tex}\left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-}{/tex} +2 Strong {tex}d^2 s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic
{tex}\left[{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}{/tex} +3 Weak {tex}s p^3 d^2{/tex} Paramagnetic
{tex}\left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right]{/tex} 0 Strong {tex}s p^3{/tex} Diamagnetic

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Discuss the nature of bonding in metal carbonyls.
question_text: Discuss the nature of bonding in metal carbonyls.
explanation: The metal carbon bond in metal carbonyls possess both S and P character. The M-C {tex}\sigma{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of lone pair of electrons on the carbonyl carbon into a vacant orbital of the metal. The M-C {tex}\pi{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of a pair of electrons form a filled d orbital of metal into the vacant anti bonding {tex}\pi^{*}{/tex} orbital of carbon monoxide. The metal to ligand bonding creates a synergic which strengthens the bond between CO and the metal. ​​​​​​​
solution: The metal carbon bond in metal carbonyls possess both S and P character. The M-C {tex}\sigma{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of lone pair of electrons on the carbonyl carbon into a vacant orbital of the metal. The M-C {tex}\pi{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of a pair of electrons form a filled d orbital of metal into the vacant anti bonding {tex}\pi^{*}{/tex} orbital of carbon monoxide. The metal to ligand bonding creates a synergic which strengthens the bond between CO and the metal. ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
1. The metal carbon bond in metal carbonyls possess both S and P character.
  2. The M-C {tex}\sigma{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of lone pair of electrons on the carbonyl carbon into a vacant orbital of the metal.
  3. The M-C {tex}\pi{/tex} bond is formed by the donation of a pair of electrons form a filled d orbital of metal into the vacant anti bonding {tex}\pi^{*}{/tex} orbital of carbon monoxide.
  4. The metal to ligand bonding creates a synergic which strengthens the bond between CO and the metal.
    ​​​​​​​

question_html: What type of isomerism is shown by the pair [Cr(H2O)6]Cl3 and [Cr(H2O)5CI] Cl2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} H2O?
question_text: What type of isomerism is shown by the pair [Cr(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 3 and [Cr(H 2 O) 5 CI] Cl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} H 2 O?

options.explanation: Solvate / Hydrate isomerism Solvate or hydrate isomers have the same composition but differ with respect to the number of solvent ligand molecules as well as the counter ion in the crystal lattice. Step I: [Cr(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 3 and [Cr(H 2 O) 5 CI]Cl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} H 2 O have same composition. Step II: In[Cr(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 3 , the number of water molecules inside the co-ordination sphere is six and the number of water molecules outside the co-ordination sphere is 0. Step III: In [Cr(H 2 O) 5 Cl]Cl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} H 2 O the number of water molecules inside the co-ordination sphere is five and the number of water molecules outside the co-ordination sphere is one.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Write the hybridization and magnetic character of following complexes:

  1. [Fe(H2O)6]2+
  2. [Fe(CO)5]

(Atomic no. of Fe = 26)
question_text: Write the hybridization and magnetic character of following complexes: [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ [Fe(CO) 5 ] (Atomic no. of Fe = 26)
explanation: In [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ the oxidation state of Fe is +2. The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d 6 4s 2 4p 0 . H 2 O is weak field ligand and do not cause pairing up of electrons. In this complex, there are 4 unpaired electrons. Therefore, it involves sp 3 d 2 hybridisation and is paramagnetic. In [Fe(CO) 5 ] the oxidation state of Fe is zero. The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d 6 4s 2 4p 0 . CO is strong field ligand, causes pairing up of 4s electrons into the 3d orbitals. In this complex, there is no unpaired electron. Therefore, it involves dsp 3 hybridization and is diamagnetic.
solution: In [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ the oxidation state of Fe is +2. The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d 6 4s 2 4p 0 . H 2 O is weak field ligand and do not cause pairing up of electrons. In this complex, there are 4 unpaired electrons. Therefore, it involves sp 3 d 2 hybridisation and is paramagnetic. In [Fe(CO) 5 ] the oxidation state of Fe is zero. The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d 6 4s 2 4p 0 . CO is strong field ligand, causes pairing up of 4s electrons into the 3d orbitals. In this complex, there is no unpaired electron. Therefore, it involves dsp 3 hybridization and is diamagnetic.
solution_html:
1. In [Fe(H2O)6]2+ the oxidation state of Fe is +2.
    The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d6 4s2 4p0. H2O is weak field ligand and do not cause pairing up of electrons.

    In this complex, there are 4 unpaired electrons. Therefore, it involves sp3d2 hybridisation and is paramagnetic.
  2. In [Fe(CO)5] the oxidation state of Fe is zero. The outermost electronic configuration of Fe is 3d64s24p0. CO is strong field ligand, causes pairing up of 4s electrons into the 3d orbitals.

    In this complex, there is no unpaired electron.
    Therefore, it involves dsp3 hybridization and is diamagnetic.

  2. [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic though both are tetrahedral. Why? (Atomic no. of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the electronic configuration of Fe(III) on the basis of crystal field theory when it forms an octahedral complex in the presence of (i) strong field ligand, and (ii) weak field ligand.
    (Atomic no. of Fe = 26)
question_text: Give one chemical test as an evidence to show that [Co(NH 3 ) 5 CI]SO 4 and [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (SO 4 )]CI are ionisation isomers. [NiCl 4 ] 2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CO) 4 ] is diamagnetic though both are tetrahedral. Why? (Atomic no. of Ni = 28) Write the electronic configuration of Fe(III) on the basis of crystal field theory when it forms an octahedral complex in the presence of (i) strong field ligand, and (ii) weak field ligand. (Atomic no. of Fe = 26)
explanation: On addition of silver nitrate, [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (SO 4 )]Cl will form white precipitate of AgCl while [Co(NH 3 ) 5 CI]SO 4 do not form any precipitate. In [Ni(CO) 4 ], Ni is in zero oxidation state whereas in [NiCl 4 ] 2- , Ni is in +2 oxidation state. In the presence of CO ligand, the unpaired d electrons of nickel pair up because CO is a strong ligand. In the presence of Cl - ligand,the unpaired d electrons of nickel are unable to pair up because Cl - is a weak ligand. So, [NiCl 4 ] 2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CO) 4 ] is diamagnetic Electronic configuration of Fe(III) in the presence of Strong field ligand is t 2 g 5 eg 0 Weak field ligand is t 2 g 3 eg 2
solution: On addition of silver nitrate, [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (SO 4 )]Cl will form white precipitate of AgCl while [Co(NH 3 ) 5 CI]SO 4 do not form any precipitate. In [Ni(CO) 4 ], Ni is in zero oxidation state whereas in [NiCl 4 ] 2- , Ni is in +2 oxidation state. In the presence of CO ligand, the unpaired d electrons of nickel pair up because CO is a strong ligand. In the presence of Cl - ligand,the unpaired d electrons of nickel are unable to pair up because Cl - is a weak ligand. So, [NiCl 4 ] 2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CO) 4 ] is diamagnetic Electronic configuration of Fe(III) in the presence of Strong field ligand is t 2 g 5 eg 0 Weak field ligand is t 2 g 3 eg 2
solution_html:
1. On addition of silver nitrate, [Co(NH3)5 (SO4)]Cl will form white precipitate of AgCl while [Co(NH3)5CI]SO4 do not form any precipitate.
  2. In [Ni(CO)4], Ni is in zero oxidation state whereas in [NiCl4]2-, Ni is in +2 oxidation state.
    In the presence of CO ligand, the unpaired d electrons of nickel pair up because CO is a strong ligand.
    In the presence of Cl- ligand,the unpaired d electrons of nickel are unable to pair up because Cl- is a weak ligand.
    So, [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic
  3. Electronic configuration of Fe(III) in the presence of
    1. Strong field ligand is t2g5 eg0
    2. Weak field ligand is t2g3 eg2

solution: Chelate complex is the complex formed when a di - or polydentate ligand uses its two or more donor atoms to bind a single metal atom. Ligand which can ligate through two different atoms is called ambidentate ligand.
solution_html:
1. Chelate complex is the complex formed when a di - or polydentate ligand uses its two or more donor atoms to bind a single metal atom.
  2. Ligand which can ligate through two different atoms is called ambidentate ligand.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Alfred Werner (1866-1919), a Swiss chemist was the first to formulate his ideas about the structures of coordination compounds. Werner proposed the concept of a primary valence and a secondary valence for a metal. The coordination entity constitutes a central metal atom or ion bonded to a fixed number of ions or molecules. In a coordination entity, the atom/ion to which a fixed number of ions/groups are bound in a definite geometrical arrangement around it is called the central atom or ion. The ions or molecules bound to the central atom/ion in the coordination entity are called ligands. Ligands may be simple ions such as Cl-, small molecules such as H2O or NH3, larger molecules such as H2NCH2CH2NH2 or N(CH2CH2NH2)3 or even macromolecules, such as protein. Ligands are unidentate, bidentate and polydentate. The coordination number (CN) of a metal ion in a complex is the number of ligand donor atoms to which the metal is directly bonded.


Amongst [Fe(C2O4)3]3- and [Fe(NH3)6]3+ which is more stable and why?
question_text: Alfred Werner (1866-1919), a Swiss chemist was the first to formulate his ideas about the structures of coordination compounds. Werner proposed the concept of a primary valence and a secondary valence for a metal. The coordination entity constitutes a central metal atom or ion bonded to a fixed number of ions or molecules. In a coordination entity, the atom/ion to which a fixed number of ions/groups are bound in a definite geometrical arrangement around it is called the central atom or ion. The ions or molecules bound to the central atom/ion in the coordination entity are called ligands. Ligands may be simple ions such as Cl - , small molecules such as H 2 O or NH 3 , larger molecules such as H 2 NCH 2 CH 2 NH 2 or N(CH 2 CH 2 NH 2 ) 3 or even macromolecules, such as protein. Ligands are unidentate, bidentate and polydentate. The coordination number (CN) of a metal ion in a complex is the number of ligand donor atoms to which the metal is directly bonded. Amongst [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- and [Fe(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ which is more stable and why?
explanation: [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- is more stable because it is a chelate. C 2 O 4 2- is a didentate ligand.
solution: [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- is more stable because it is a chelate. C 2 O 4 2- is a didentate ligand.
solution_html: [Fe(C2O4)3]3- is more stable because it is a chelate. C2O42- is a didentate ligand.

  2. Why [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN)4]2- is diamagnetic? (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes rarely observed?
question_text: What type of isomerism is shown by the complex [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (SCN)] 2+ ? Why [NiCl 4 ] 2- is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- is diamagnetic? (Atomic number of Ni = 28) Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes rarely observed?
explanation: [Co(NH3)5(SCN)] 2+ exhibit linkage isomerism due to the presence of SCN which is an ambidentate ligand and can link with metal either through N or S. [Co(NH 3 ) 5 SCN] 2+ and [Co(NH 3 ) 5 NCS] 2+ The complex in which one or more unpaired electrons are present is a paramagnetic while, those which does not contain any unpaired electron is diamagnetic. Ni atom (Z = 28) Oxidation state of Ni is +2in both the complexes i.e. [NiCl 4 ] 2- and [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- {tex}\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}{/tex} = In the case of [NiCl 4 ] 2- , Cl - is a weak field ligand so the pairing of electrons in 3d-orbital does not occur, hence compound is paramagnetic with two unpaired electrons. In [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- , CN - is a strong field ligand, hence pairing occurs and {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}{/tex} is diamagnetic. For tetrahedral complexes, the crystal field stabilization energy is lower than pairing energy, so they are rarely formed in the low spin state.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. What type of isomerism is shown by the complex [Co(NH3)6] [Cr(CN)6]?
  2. Why a solution of [Ni(H2O)6]2+ is green while a solution of [Ni(CN)4]2- is colourless? (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex : [Co(NH3)5(CO3)]Cl.
question_text: What type of isomerism is shown by the complex [Co(NH 3 ) 6] [Cr(CN) 6 ]? Why a solution of [Ni(H 2 O)6] 2+ is green while a solution of [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- is colourless? (Atomic number of Ni = 28) Write the IUPAC name of the following complex : [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (CO 3 )]Cl.
explanation: The type of isomerism exhibited by the [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ) [Cr(CN) 6 ) is coordination isomerism. Electronic configuration of Ni (Z = 28) = [Ar] 3d 8 4s 2 Ni atom(Z = 28) = The oxidation state of Ni is +2 in both [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ and [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- . {tex}[\mathrm{Ni}]^{2+}{/tex} = In the case of [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ , Hp is a weak field ligand so the pairing of electrons in 3d-orbital does not occur. These unpaired electrons get excited from a lower energy d-orbital to a higher energy d-orbital. Due to d-d transition, [Ni(Hp) 6 ] 2+ possesses green colour, whereas CN - is a strong field ligand so pairing occurs and a solution of [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- is colorless. The IUPAC name of the complex [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (CO 3 )]Cl is Penta ammine carbonatocobalt (III) chloride.
solution: The type of isomerism exhibited by the [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ) [Cr(CN) 6 ) is coordination isomerism. Electronic configuration of Ni (Z = 28) = [Ar] 3d 8 4s 2 Ni atom(Z = 28) = The oxidation state of Ni is +2 in both [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ and [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- . {tex}[\mathrm{Ni}]^{2+}{/tex} = In the case of [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ , Hp is a weak field ligand so the pairing of electrons in 3d-orbital does not occur. These unpaired electrons get excited from a lower energy d-orbital to a higher energy d-orbital. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Define crystal field splitting energy. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} < P.
  2. [Ni(CN)4]2- is colourless whereas [Ni(H2O)6]2+ is green. Why? (At. no. of Ni = 28)
question_text: Define crystal field splitting energy. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d 4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} < P. [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- is colourless whereas [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ is green. Why? (At. no. of Ni = 28)
explanation: Crystal field splitting energy is the energy difference between two sets of d-orbital levels that split when ligands approach a transition metal ion. If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} , it becomes more energetically favourable for the {tex}d^4{/tex} election to occupy a {tex}t_{2 g}{/tex} orbital with configuration {tex}t_{2 g} ^4e_g^0{/tex} . {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^2{/tex} is colounless whereas {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}{/tex} is green, because In both complexes, Ni is in +2 oxidation state with electronic configuration of {tex}3 {~d}^8{/tex} . In the presence of weak field water ligands, two unpaired electrons do not pair up. Hence, the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}{\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} has two unpaired electrons which result in green colour compound. Due to d-d transition, red light is absorbed and complimentary light emitted is green. In presence of strong field cyanide ligand, the unpaired electrons in 3d orbital pair up. Due to an absence of unpaired electrons and no d-d transitions are possible and the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}({CN})_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} ends up as colourless compound.
solution: Crystal field splitting energy is the energy difference between two sets of d-orbital levels that split when ligands approach a transition metal ion. If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex} , it becomes more energetically favourable for the {tex}d^4{/tex} election to occupy a {tex}t_{2 g}{/tex} orbital with configuration {tex}t_{2 g} ^4e_g^0{/tex} . {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^2{/tex} is colounless whereas {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}{/tex} is green, because In both complexes, Ni is in +2 oxidation state with electronic configuration of {tex}3 {~d}^8{/tex} . In the presence of weak field water ligands, two unpaired electrons do not pair up. Hence, the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}{\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} has two unpaired electrons which result in green colour compound. Due to d-d transition, red light is absorbed and complimentary light emitted is green. In presence of strong field cyanide ligand, the unpaired electrons in 3d orbital pair up. Due to an absence of unpaired electrons and no d-d transitions are possible and the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}({CN})_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} ends up as colourless compound.
solution_html:
1. Crystal field splitting energy is the energy difference between two sets of d-orbital levels that split when ligands approach a transition metal ion.
    If {tex}\Delta_0>P{/tex}, it becomes more energetically favourable for the {tex}d^4{/tex} election to occupy a {tex}t_{2 g}{/tex} orbital with configuration {tex}t_{2 g} ^4e_g^0{/tex}.
  2. {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^2{/tex} is colounless whereas
    {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}{/tex} is green, because
    In both complexes, Ni is in +2 oxidation state with electronic configuration of {tex}3 {~d}^8{/tex}.
    In the presence of weak field water ligands, two unpaired electrons do not pair up.
    Hence, the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}{\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} has two unpaired electrons which result in green colour compound.
    Due to d-d transition, red light is absorbed and complimentary light emitted is green.
    In presence of strong field cyanide ligand, the unpaired electrons in 3d orbital pair up.
    Due to an absence of unpaired electrons and no d-d transitions are possible and the complex {tex}\left[{Ni}({CN})_4\right]^{2-}{/tex} ends up as colourless compound.

question_html:
When a coordination compound CoCl3 ·6NH3 is mixed with AgNO3, 3 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write

  1. structural formula of the complex.
  2. IUPAC name of the complex.
question_text: When a coordination compound CoCl 3 ·6NH 3 is mixed with AgNO 3 , 3 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write structural formula of the complex. IUPAC name of the complex.
explanation: When one mole of CoCl 3 .6NH 3 is mixed with AgNO 3 , three moles of AgCl are precipitated which indicates that three ionisable chloride ions in the complex are present. Hence, its structural formula is [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 . IUPAC name of the complex [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 is Hexa ammine cobalt (III) chloride.
solution: When one mole of CoCl 3 .6NH 3 is mixed with AgNO 3 , three moles of AgCl are precipitated which indicates that three ionisable chloride ions in the complex are present. Hence, its structural formula is [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 . IUPAC name of the complex [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 is Hexa ammine cobalt (III) chloride.
